## [15:18 - 18:23]

lot more controlling of what's going on. So, there's two things to just bear in mind about manual bid. Because the auction will change throughout the day and day by day, you need to be on top of, do you want CPA or do you want volume? Because one day you will see that a max bid of, let's say, 20 works well. The next day is just no traffic or there's no conversions that come through. So, that's one thing that the ideal bid will change daily. It will change hourly. And so, you may win or lose some auctions. So, this is where I prefer to have a range of max bids that I'm working towards so that if I am seeing an opportunity, I can put more budget in one or I just pause it and kind of pull it back. So, that's just one thing to bear in mind with that. The second thing is the more expensive the auction, sorry, the higher the manual bid you're going for, the more expensive the user and therefore the higher the cost per cost per click also goes up. In theory, when the cost per click goes up, your conversion rate should also go up because you're attracting better quality customers. The problem is that doesn't always happen. And so, in your case, for example, the CPC has gone up, you're reaching a more expensive audience. But if they're converting at the same rate, then it's not working out and you need to either cut that ad set or pause it down. And this is one of the important things about scaling is it's just not, it's not the normal thing to just continue scaling your spend. There are some accounts where you can do that and there's constant growth. But generally, because of the auction being so unpredictable, you need to be able to react. So, for example, on the module that I'm working on for automations, I had rules set at certain parts of the day so that if the performance doesn't hit, then we're either pausing the ad set or pausing ads, but then restarting them the following day as well. Because sometimes you have to accept that the auction is not your friend today and you have to then pull back your spend and then move up again. And I think when I see a lot of people working in Facebook ads, they're not appreciating that it's not always going to be consistent. It's really difficult to make it consistent. But what you can do is get consistent results by being on top of that performance. So, for example, here, this particular ad set that you've got highlighted, if the CPC is increasing, the auction is getting more expensive. I would also be looking at the ads themselves. ## [24:26 - 27:27]

So question from Joe. When on bottom of funnel dynamic product ads, would you generally turn on all most placements, excluding Instagram? What do you normally do? Also the minimum budget, would you take on a client or start a store for a brand new store, a new pixel? When is my slot? Now's your slot. So bottom of funnel, DPA. I would leave the placements on because it's a hot audience, especially if it's bottom of funnel. If you're doing DPA on cold, then no, I wouldn't. The reason for keeping on placements when it's a hot audience, when they know you're ready is, and this is just my experience, and it's something you should test, is the more times they see your ad, the more chance they are, the more chance they're going to kind of convert when they see the ad in the right placement. So I've done tests where I've just run in the newsfeed, for example, versus including Facebook audience network, and especially with warm, sorry, hot audiences, just by pushing those impressions out, it improves the performance. So if you were to look at placement by placement, Facebook audience network would have a really bad cost-pracrosition, for example, but when you look at the blended, it looks better. So in my experience, the DPA is obviously just specific formats, but when I'm running generally with hot audiences, I prefer to leave all the slots on, unless I'm really tight on budget and I don't want too much wasted on audience network or right-hand side, for example, but I think the DPA, I would leave it on, the minimum budget to take on a client or start a store for a brand new store, not exactly sure. So I think what you're asking is, if I was to take on a client, what would I ask for their minimum budget to be? I don't ask for a minimum budget, I have a minimum fee. So for example, if a client absolutely wants to work with me and my agency, and even if they're spending £100 a day, but they want to use the first two to four weeks to test and then they're ready to scale up after that, then we accept them. So it's more what's their forward strategy. If it's a brand new store, no pixel, no nothing, for me, when I take on a new client, I have a 30-day clause because it works two ways. If we can't find you traction or your site's not converting, there's no point tying up into any kind of long-term contract. So the first 30 days is always discovery. One thing that I've learned is if the site is brand new, if there's no pixel, etc, you absolutely need to set the expectation right from the start, that it could easily take a month before you're ready to see some really positive results because there's so many things that still could go wrong. Maybe the site's not converting, maybe there's a checkout problem, maybe we still haven't found the right creative, etc. So I think there are just a few things that I'd caveat. The perfect clients are the ones that already have conversions. So you can already start to say,
